item.title: Re: Necro-Post I know
item.content:encoded:
A little bit more quantifiable way of discerning what to call a gun is:

  • Less than 20mm/0.79in bore diameter = small arm
  • 20mm or more = Destructive Device or Heavy Machine Gun (Depending on projectile)
  • Gun's barrel length measured in calibers(the bore diameter of the shell) = Artillery

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know I'm necro-ing a year old post here, but judging from the date of the article on the GOC archive, I'd imagine that it's also written by Aelanna. Problem is that we can't be sure as they haven't been active since 2015.

item.title: Re: Necro-Post I know
item.content:encoded:
To clarify, small arms are any firearm that one person can carry and use. "Small" as opposed to light weapons1 and field artillery2.

Footnotes
1. it takes more than one person to carry it and the ammo and/or it involves the words "blast radius"
2. It probably has wheels and a dedicated crew.

item.title: Re: Necro-Post I know
item.content:encoded:
…

  1. Not a necro. You can comment on any SCP's discussion page. You're good.
  2. A .30-06 is a small arm. I'm not seeing the issue here.

item.title: Necro-Post I know
item.content:encoded: I don’t know if this was on purpose or not but the G.O.C letter says the Type Green was terminated by small arms fire yet the bullet belongs to an M2 Armor Piercing .30-06 Springfield rifle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wouldn't it be okay to bring back the old GOC report for this, archive it on this website, and have this profile link to it?

edit: I can't figure out who wrote it. Does anyone here know who did?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the idea of this one. Straight to the point, professional and it even has an interesting tie-in to the GOC and a cool tale.

Also, God bless the GOC.

item.title: Matrix
item.content:encoded: How to kill Neo

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh crap, Sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
AnticMaestro, this is the discussion page for an SCP, which has no relation to in-universe Kondraki nor the author Kondraki.

Also, if you're referring to the tale that references this SCP, that one was written by TroyL.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Uhhhh…… I think you posted to the wrong discussion page, mate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Damn, this is one of my favorite Tales in the Foundation. Kondraki was one of my Favorite Seniors in the Foundation, He was that Badass Bulldog who kept People in Line. (Spoilers?) I re-read The History of the Universe Hub, All of them in fact, and i have to admit i was shocked about his little Antics and the fact that he actually abused his Power. Overall Good Tale, Simple and Exciting.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this one is brilliant. so short, so simple, but again so many implications apply that the item itself becomes it's own story. That guy could've been a possible SCP, but in a way it feels as if we were too late. there's a feeling of guilt to this. when you pull off a minimalistic style well, it sticks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is just enough to be this short and this good

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's not much meat to this. It's tiny, tight and compact. I liked the containment procedures, but the actual wording hit my ear wrong. I think it's the overuse of passive voice? I think that would do well with some editing, but that minor quibble isn't enough to keep me from upvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The GOC report in conjunction with this is no longer on the page linked here. It just says TRANSFER COMPLETE. Can anyone tell me where it's been transferred?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That report at the end was hilarious.

Well... consider another side of the story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would love to read the full GOC report behind this. That report at the end was hilarious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: i liked the crosslinking, it's a matter of taste i think

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I keep going back and forth from +1 to -1, and these comments all make valid points. in the end my vote is +1 based on the professionalism of the article rather than a feeling about the subject.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Short and to the point! People should take notes!
Been wanting this one to reach 100 for a while. Congrats, Aelanna.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Short and sweet. SCPs like this show that a work doesn't have to be paragraphs upon paragraphs in length to be good and effective.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I know what you could add to this.

I mean, if the GOC learned about this "Clockwork kid" by watching the news, and it was a major event like it says there, surely someone in the foundation watched that same newscast.

Someone must make the connection.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do like this (especially the bit about how it was stopped by another (potential) SCP) - but I think its a bit more of an anomalous object considering it doesn't do anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a great short and simple article. I can understand the similarities with 1782 but they are very superficial.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Happened to me in Fallout once.

I don't like this one much, and not just because it reminds me of that one time in Fallout. I'm not sure the crossover does it for me, and I'd probably prefer to get more of the backstory here, rather than having to follow a link in the discussion of the SCP itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Found this via Troy's excellent tale about it. It's excellent. +1'ed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Could be a different numbering system, where the number means something to someone (first digit is the level of danger, or year found, etc.)

Different criteria of things - where the Foundation has anomalous Items, maybe they list ALL of them in one list.

Maybe they I.D. things by incident, then merge it all back into one number. "This observation report from the Rocky Mountains lists it as 4852. It turns out to be the same thing from the Ganges Desert where it was listed as 1335!" "Okay, re-designate both of them as 9517 and put them n the same file. Then kill it."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We only catalogue what we've contained. GOC catalogues everything they know about/kills. I think the latter is easier.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it just me, or is no one else disturbed by the fact that they called it 3410? Does that mean that the GOC has files on/knows about/is hunting nearly more than TWICE the number of our SCPs?

item.title: Jawesome!
item.content:encoded: Huh, you learn something new every day!

item.title: Incorrect.
item.content:encoded: Even heavy machine guns that require multiple personnel for deployment and operation are classified as such. Most militaries and doctrinal overviews draw the line at modern definitions of cannon of any sort.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My first SCP (terrible, by the way) had that idea in mind (not that it got across). It was a slingshot that fired an extremely fast-moving pellet, but it turned out that the pellet was completely still in relation to the universe and our extremely fast movement made it look like it was moving so rapidly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes. Generally, if it can be carried around and operated by a single infantryman, it counts as small arms.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Keep in mind that there is no universal frame of reference. Anything that's completely stopped has to be stopped in relation to something else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … the bullet continues on its path and blasts a hole in the floor. Simple.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does a high-powered rifle really qualify as "small arms?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The sell is that IT KILLED A REALITY BENDER BY GOING THROUGH ITS FACE!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I completely understand that not every SCP has be to dangerous or creepy - in fact, I generally prefer "weird" to "creepy". But compared to the many quality ideas here, I don't think a floating bullet is interesting enough on its own. I think it need something to sell it. A vague danger is one way of fixing that. That or adding something more unusual than the fact that it is suspended in the same spot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What makes this interesting for me, is that is it only suspended in time (staying in mid-air), or space as well? Does it "move" in the opposite direction of the Earth's rotation, i.e. stay in place as everything else rotates? Does it stay in the same position in space as the Earth revolves around the sun or move along with it?

EDIT: Of course it says in the article that it stays in the same spot connected to the Earth, but I found that "what if?" thought interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am getting increasingly disappointed in newbies who believe that only 'creepy' or 'dangerous' things get to be SCPs. The primary mission of the Foundation is to protect humanity, yes, but a huge secondary mission to that is the preservation of normalcy. It doesn't have to be creepy or dangerous to cause worldwide panic if the existence of the anomalous were to be disseminated. So, it's contained to let the sheeple of the world remain oblivious to just how little we control our destinies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 1899 needs a threat. As it is, it's just a bullet suspended in mid-air. Weird but not exactly high on the list of things that need close monitoring. Perhaps it rotates, following an unknown target, without moving from the spot. Perhaps it has been observed creeping forward at an ever increase rate? Something that justifies giving a darn what it does.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What the bullet itself does for me is, what happens if the temporal stasis wears off?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked this before discovering it actually has a backstory. I'm afraid I'm giving it only one upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For once, you and I disagree Drew. I actually like this MORE with the other side of the coin; for me it's kind of like the odd (non-massive-company-crossover-event) bit where something in DC is referenced in Marvel, or the other way around. Gives a bit of fanboism, I admit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok, makes a bit more sense put that way. I know that 1782 is the room, not the bender, but it's hard to think of it that way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Re-read it and you're right. It IS just the bullet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This… actually makes me like this SCP even less. Now it's officially a supplement to a GOC tale-ish thing, rather than being sufficient to stand-alone. Don't get me wrong, I find the interaction that you set up between the two groups to be very well done, but I just can't get around the fact that this page is supplement to something else.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I got the impression that it was only the bullet itself that couldn't be sampled. The residue on it was still normal (relatively speaking).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you can't get samples how would you know it's brain matter?

Also, are the GOC files setup for migration?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm sorry… can somebody explain this one to me, as in why did this get so many thumbs? Because there is a dead dude that slows time..? Maybe because the bullet is still suspended for no particular reason? Any pointers appreciated

EDIT: Now I see there is some kind of a follow up story needed, that is nice, but as a whole scp, this gets a downvote from me…

Ok, read the story, downvote deleted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 1782 is an anomalous room created by the death (or attempted killing of) a reality bender, so there's overlap. I think these are different though, this is basically an anomalous crime scene while 1782 is still ongoing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A scp should be supplemented by a story. Not the other way around. Down voted for being a short boring tie in.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do feel like 1782 did this better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Funny story...

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I'd like it better if it were moving very slowly, instead of just suspended in one spot indefinitely, but, I dunno. It works for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting SCP with a GOC link?
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I love simple, short SCPs, this one's almost too simple. But it does work for what it sets out to accomplish, so upvote.
